hypothesis: With the `gated` mainline anchored back at `K=128, EF=12, optimizer=signum, lr=1.6e-3`, adding a small auxiliary sparsity-support loss via `auxk_alpha=0.03125` may improve training dynamics without changing the family or optimizer; this round asks only whether a light loss-shaping term reduces plateauing and yields a better full-curve profile than the plain baseline.
summary: The optimizer branch already failed once (`adam` at the matched LR), and the follow-up Adam LR callback was policy-rejected, so the next valid mainline move is to return to the proven `signum` anchor and test one loss-side variable. Keep architecture, K, EF, optimizer, and LR fixed, and introduce a small `auxk_alpha` rather than a large perturbation. Evaluate the whole trajectory: if early progress is steadier and the run finishes at or below the current `0.149866`, loss shaping is worth keeping in play; if the curve is uniformly worse or distorts convergence, drop aux loss and preserve the plain gated recipe.
self_review: This stays on the same family, keeps EF fixed at 12, does not touch K, and changes exactly one main variable: `auxk_alpha`. It also respects the prescribed order by moving to a loss-side probe only after the basic LR and optimizer checks have not yielded an improvement.
notes_to_memory: Round 34 returns to the gated+signum anchor and begins the loss-side probe with a small aux term: AUXK_ALPHA=0.03125.
Judge this run by curve shape as well as endpoint; if aux loss does not help the trajectory or finish, drop it and avoid broad loss sweeps.
next_hypotheses: If `auxk_alpha=0.03125` improves the curve or endpoint, test one nearby aux setting to see whether the benefit is robust or over-tuned.
If the aux-loss run is clearly worse, revert to the plain gated anchor and only then consider a preprocess-side change before touching K.
